exemplar.title: To what extent are maternal infections related to developing sensorineural hearing loss in children?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent are maternal infections related to developing sensorineural hearing loss in children?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and effective communication of the topic with appropriate use of key terminology
Research question is explicitly stated, focused, and sustained through most of the essay
Methodology includes relevant primary (interviews) and secondary (literature) approaches with basic rationale
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into peripheral topics dilute focus
Methodological justification lacks detail on sample selection, variable control and data-analysis procedures
Some repetition and minor imprecision in explanations limit depth of communication
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work consistently meets the descriptors for the 3â4 band. Two strands (topic communication and research question) demonstrate strong performance at the top of that band, while the methodology strand sits at the lower end due to limited justification. An overall score of 4 reflects solid but not fully comprehensive focus and method.

strengths: Accurate and context-appropriate use of technical vocabulary (e.g., vestibulocochlear nerve, syncytiotrophoblast)
Integration of primary interview data enriches the investigation and demonstrates engagement with multiple source types
weaknesses: Inconsistent formatting of scientific notation (cytokines) and occasional imprecise phrasing
Frequent missing or incomplete citations, limiting verification of percentage figures and statistical claims
Limited critical evaluation of sources and absence of source reliability assessment
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance aligns with the mid-band 3â4 descriptors: terminology and application of source material are generally sound but lack consistency and depth. A score of 3 accurately reflects mostly relevant use of sources and mostly accurate use of concepts without fully effective implementation.

strengths: Analysis remains focused on the research question, linking interview testimony with literature findings
Discussion acknowledges methodological limitations and alternative factors, demonstrating good evaluative intent
Argument is generally coherent with logical progression through background, data presentation, and conclusions
weaknesses: Analysis is largely descriptive with no statistical testing or in-depth treatment of confounding variables
Evaluation of evidence and methodological reliability is superficial rather than fully critical
Argument occasionally drifts into narrative description and repeats content, weakening overall coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands are firmly in the lower end of the 4â6 band: analysis and evaluation are present but lack sophistication, and argument development is clear but not consistently rigorous. An overall score of 4 reflects competent but not advanced critical thinking.

strengths: Logical use of standard sections and headings provides general structural appropriateness
High-quality anatomical diagrams and tables enhance reader comprehension
weaknesses: Missing front-matter elements (word count, page numbers) and inconsistent figure/table numbering
Redundancies and uneven section lengths indicate incomplete application of layout guidelines
Mixed citation styles and formatting inconsistencies detract from professional presentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation falls within the 1â2 band: structure is generally appropriate but marred by missing required elements, and layout/formatting conventions are only partially applied. A score of 2 reflects these combined strengths and shortcomings.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report employs standard sections (introduction, methodology, discussion) and uses headings to guide the reader, demonstrating **general structural appropriateness**. Nonetheless, there is noticeable redundancy, uneven section lengths, and the absence of some required front-matter (word count) weakens overall organisation. These issues place the work in the **upper end of the 1-2 band (score = 2)**.

While the student includes figures, tables, and headings, **key formatting conventions are missing or inconsistently applied**: no page numbers, no initial word count, irregular figure numbering, and mixed citation styles. Some captions are detailed, others minimal. These shortcomings indicate only **partial adherence to layout guidelines**, corresponding to the lower mark in the 1-2 band (score = 1).

The essay follows a logical sequenceâbackground, specific infections, data presentation, conclusionsâand integrates counter-arguments (e.g., genetic causes). However, **arguments sometimes drift into descriptive narrative**, and link-back to the research question weakens in extended background sections. Occasional repetition and limited synthesis of evidence impede coherence. These factors indicate a **clear but not fully consistent argument (score = 4, 4-6 band).**

The student provides an informative overview of sensorineural hearing loss and maternal infections, using clear language and appropriate supporting data (e.g., WHO prevalence figures, anatomical diagram). Key terms such as âsensorineural hearing loss,â âsyncytiotrophoblast,â and specific pathogens are introduced with definitions, showing **sound but not fully comprehensive explanation**. Some repetition, minor factual imprecision, and occasional lapses in clarity limit the depth of communication, placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band rather than the highest level.

The essay integrates WHO statistics, CDC data, interview excerpts and academic definitions, demonstrating **mostly relevant application** of sources. Nevertheless, several mechanistic claims (âGoogle scholarâ) lack proper citation, some figures are reproduced without critical commentary, and source evaluation is absent. This limits effectiveness, so the strand fits the **mid-level (3) of the 3-4 band**.

The student conducts **some analysis** of survey data (e.g., linking infection timing to hearing outcomes) and triangulates interview testimony with literature. Yet, absence of statistical testing, limited treatment of confounders, and reliance on descriptive commentary constrain depth. The analysis is clearly related to the question but **lacks sophistication and rigour**, placing it in the **lower end of the 4-6 band (score = 4)**.

An interview study (20 mothers, several clinicians) and literature review are proposed; however, **rationale for sample size, selection criteria, and data-analysis techniques (e.g., coding, statistics) remains thin**. Ethical considerations are mentioned but not documented, and variables/confounders are not fully controlled. The chosen methods are relevant yet lack the detailed justification and completeness expected for the top bands, fitting better within the **lower end of the 3-4 band (score = 3)**.

The discussion recognises limitations (small sample, need for larger datasets) and touches on alternative factors (genetics, prematurity). Conclusions are **generally supported by presented evidence**, but evaluation of methodology, reliability of sources, and statistical significance is cursory. The evaluative component is thus competent yet **not consistently deep or critical**, meriting a **score at the bottom of the 4-6 band**.

The research question â âTo what extent are maternal infections related to developing sensorineural hearing loss in children?â â is **explicitly stated and appropriately narrow for a Biology EE**. Throughout most sections the student attempts to keep discussion tied to this focus, but occasional digressions into general paediatric health and legal issues dilute precision. The link between each sub-section and the central question is therefore only partially sustained, corresponding to the 3-4 band.

Technical vocabularyâe.g., *vestibulocochlear nerve*, *IL-17*, *syncytiotrophoblast*âis used **largely accurately and in context**, indicating a reasonable grasp of biological concepts. However, inconsistent formatting of cytokine notation and occasional imprecise phrasing (âhyper bilirubinemiaâ) reveal gaps in consistency. Overall performance aligns with the **3-4 band (score = 3)**.

annotations.comment: The title and research question section lack the required initial word count, which is a formatting requirement for this extended essay.
The personal anecdote is engaging but could be condensed to maintain focus on scientific rationale and avoid digressions.
The transition into the background section is abrupt; adding a linking sentence would improve overall coherence.
The detailed immunological account demonstrates understanding but would benefit from clearer linkage to specific impacts on fetal auditory development.
This complex immunological description could be supported by a schematic or table to make the information more accessible and digestible.
The title is concise and accurately conveys the investigationâs focus on maternal infections and offspring hearing loss, establishing a clear thematic anchor for the essay.
The research question is clearly stated and appropriately focused, providing a strong and specific basis for the investigation.
Listing multiple perinatal and genetic risk factors illustrates breadth but would benefit from critical evaluation of their relative impact on hearing loss.
Restating the research question clarifies the aim, but repeating it verbatim reduces space for analytical introduction. Consider focusing more on rationale here.
The use of WHO statistics demonstrates application of reputable sources but lacks specific citation details (e.g., report date or document title) for verification.
This section provides a clear explanation of sensorineural hearing loss mechanisms, effectively using domain terminology to demonstrate solid understanding.
The methodology section mentions interviews and secondary sources but omits sample size rationale, variable control and data analysis procedures, weakening methodological rigor.
The statement on severe outcomes repeats earlier content; streamlining this repetition will improve conciseness and maintain focus.
The anatomical diagram is detailed and accurately labelled, significantly enhancing reader comprehension of internal ear structures.
The explanation of sensorineural hearing loss here repeats earlier content; consider consolidating to avoid redundancy and maintain engagement.
The integration of primary interview data with clinical context enriches the investigation, illustrating effective use of qualitative sources.
This description of prenatal risk factors blends distinct causes; clearly separating genetic from infection-related risks would improve clarity.
The section header clearly delineates the topic focus, aiding navigation and reinforcing the essayâs structural organization.
While legal accountability context is informative, its direct relevance to sensorineural hearing loss is limited; consider refocusing on biological mechanisms.
This clinical insight is valuable, but would be strengthened by quantifying incidence rates or referencing specific case studies for context.
Highlighting the 2022 surge is pertinent, yet discussion of potential causes (e.g., lapses in vaccination) would deepen analytical insight.
The detailed Rubella mechanism shows depth of knowledge but percentage risk figures (e.g., 90%) need explicit, peer-reviewed citations for support.
Presenting WHO case data is relevant, but lacks deeper analysis of how incidence trends correlate with vaccination policies or hearing outcomes.
The introduction to neonatal infection categories lists many conditions; grouping them in a table or thematic clusters would enhance readability.
The decade-long table charts rubella incidence effectively, providing a clear visual of trends over time.
Guidance on speech milestones is informative but drifts from the maternal infection focus; consider relocating it to clinical implications in discussion.
The stacked bar chart is well-designed, but the absence of cited data sources or numeric annotations limits interpretive precision.
The Zika transmission diagram is informative, but dense labels and small font may hinder reader comprehension; consider simplifying visuals.
The cytokine listing (IL-6, IL-15, IFN-Î±, etc.) lacks proper LaTeX formatting and a peer-reviewed citation; replace vague 'Google scholar' with specific references.
The CMV description outlines basic facts but misses explicit connections to hearing loss pathophysiology; expanding on cochlear impact would strengthen the argument.
The explanation of syncytiotrophoblast functions demonstrates clear understanding of placental defense mechanisms.
While fetal vulnerability in the first trimester is well-articulated, explicitly linking these vulnerabilities to specific stages of ear development is needed.
The description of otoscopic examination is informative, but referencing standard audiological protocols would strengthen methodological validity.
The survey data table presents valuable primary data but mixed formatting of response entries reduces clarity; standardize cell formats for readability.
Summary percentages reinforce findings, yet statistical analysis (e.g., chi-square test) would provide more robust support for observed correlations.
The pie chart effectively illustrates causal proportions in childhood hearing loss, clearly emphasizing the leading role of maternal infections.
The concluding synthesis reiterates key findings but could be more concise by focusing on novel insights rather than restating prior points.
